name: Irish Atlantic Coast

staple_foods.name: Irish grass-fed butter

primary_metabolites.lipid_content: 80-84 g/100g [Kerrygold-style Irish grass-fed butter — Collomb et al. 2002 J Dairy Sci 85:1239]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 22-26% of total fatty acids [Irish grass-fed butter — Collomb et al. 2002]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 1.8-2.6% of total fatty acids [Irish grass-fed butter — Collomb et al. 2002]
primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 26-30% of total fatty acids [Irish grass-fed butter — Collomb et al. 2002]
primary_metabolites.stearic_acid: 10-12% of total fatty acids [Irish grass-fed butter]

key_flavor_bioactives.conjugated_linoleic_acid: 2.0-3.5 g/100g fat [Irish grass-fed butter CLA — Collomb et al. 2002 J Dairy Sci 85:1239; elevated vs grain-fed but lower than alpine; Atlantic maritime grass diet]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 20-40 mg/kg [Irish grass-fed butter tocopherol — moderate; Collomb et al. 2002]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 5-15 mg GAE/100g [Irish grass-fed butter — trace polyphenols from grass]

staple_foods.cultivar: Bos taurus (Friesian, Irish Atlantic grass-fed, lowland ~100m)
staple_foods.notes: Irish Atlantic grass-fed butter — the CLA midpoint in the butter natural experiment. Year-round grazing on Atlantic maritime ryegrass-clover pastures at sea level produces elevated CLA (2.0-3.5 g/100g fat) vs grain-fed Nordic lowland butter (RSU-43) but lower than alpine summer-pasture butter (RSU-36). The Atlantic climate with mild temperatures and high rainfall produces high-quality grass but without the elevation-driven herb diversity of the Alps. Natural experiment group: RSU-36 (alpine, 1200m, ~3-5.5 g CLA), RSU-37 (Irish Atlantic, 100m, ~2-3.5 g CLA), RSU-43 (Nordic lowland, 30m, ~0.6-1.5 g CLA). The altitude-forage gradient is the clean terroir variable.
data_sources: Collomb et al. (2002) — Conjugated linoleic acids and trans fatty acids in milk fat, J Dairy Sci 85:1239
Leiber et al. (2005) — Effect of high altitude grazing on milk composition, J Dairy Sci 88:1316

staple_foods.name: Atlantic salmon

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 18-22% of total fatty acids [Atlantic farmed salmon — Joensen & Torstensen 2010 Aquacult Nutr 16:283]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 10-15% of total fatty acids [farmed Atlantic salmon — varies with diet; Joensen & Torstensen 2010]

key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 2.0-4.0 mg/100g [salmon — Joensen & Torstensen 2010; among highest of any common food per 100g serving]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.20-0.32 g/100g free glutamate [salmon muscle — Maga 1983 estimate for fatty fish]
umami_compounds.inosinate: 0.40-0.80 g/100g IMP [salmon — high IMP; Nishimura & Kato 1988 J Food Sci 53:1217]

staple_foods.cultivar: Salmo salar (Atlantic salmon, Irish Sea / North Atlantic)
staple_foods.notes: Atlantic salmon — the defining protein of the Irish Atlantic coast. High omega-3 (DHA/EPA) fat content makes salmon the richest marine fat source in the dataset. The inosinate (IMP) content is among the highest of any fish, contributing to the characteristic savory/umami richness of fresh salmon. Wild vs farmed fatty acid profiles differ significantly — this RSU uses Atlantic farmed as the more consistent data source.
data_sources: Joensen & Torstensen (2010) — Fatty acid composition of farmed Atlantic salmon, Aquacult Nutr 16:283
Nishimura & Kato (1988) — Taste compounds in meat extracts, J Food Sci 53:1217

staple_foods.name: oatmeal (rolled oats)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 3.2-4.1% of total fatty acids [oat lipid — largely linoleic-dominant; Peterson 2001 Cereal Chem 78:119]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 36-43% of total fatty acids [oat lipid — linoleic dominant among the cereals; Peterson 2001]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 600-1000 mg GAE/100g [oat avenanthramides + phenolic acids — Sur et al. 2015 J Food Sci 80:C1]

staple_foods.notes: Oats are the defining grain of the Irish Atlantic food culture — porridge (stirabout) was historically the primary breakfast starch for millennia. The Atlantic maritime climate (cool, wet summers) is ideal for oat cultivation. Oats have the highest fat content of any cereal and a distinctive avenanthramide polyphenol not found in other grains.
data_sources: Peterson (2001) — Oat lipid composition, Cereal Chem 78:119
Sur et al. (2015) — Oat avenanthramides, J Food Sci 80:C1

staple_foods.name: dulse / sea vegetables (Palmaria palmata)

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 8-15 g/100g dry weight [dulse — Mæhre et al. 2014 J Agric Food Chem 62:9371]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 80-300 mg GAE/100g [dulse — phlorotannins; Mæhre et al. 2014]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.8-3.5 g/100g dry weight [dulse free glutamate — Mouritsen et al. 2012 Flavour 1:4; exceptionally high for a plant food — natural umami powerhouse]

staple_foods.notes: Dulse (dillisk) — the iconic Irish Atlantic seaweed, traditionally eaten dried along the western coast. The free glutamate content of dulse (1.8-3.5 g/100g dry weight) is exceptionally high for a plant food — comparable to tomato paste and fermented foods. This explains the traditional use of dulse as a flavoring/condiment. Palmaria palmata also has a unique fatty acid profile with omega-3 EPA.
data_sources: Mæhre et al. (2014) — Protein, amino acids, and antioxidants in Norwegian seaweeds, J Agric Food Chem 62:9371
Mouritsen et al. (2012) — Umami from the sea, Flavour 1:4

staple_foods.name: Irish whiskey (pot still, LAB/yeast fermented grain)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: trace [whiskey — minimal polyphenol in distilled spirit vs beer/wine]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: trace [whiskey distillate — LAB sour mash fermentation precursor]
organic_acids.acetic_acid: 0.05-0.2 g/L [Irish whiskey acetic acid — ester precursor in pot still distillation; Conner et al. 1998]

staple_foods.notes: Irish pot still whiskey — triple distilled, unpeated, using mixed malted/unmalted barley in copper pot stills. Placed in fermented slot as whiskey production (grain LAB sour mash fermentation + distillation) is the defining Irish fermented food/beverage tradition. The pot still character creates a creamy, fruity ester profile distinct from Scottish malt whisky (peated) or Bourbon (new oak). Organic acids are precursors to flavor esters in the distillate.
data_sources: Conner et al. (1998) — Cogener production during Scotch whisky production, J Inst Brew 104:287

notes: Galway / Connemara / County Clare area, Irish Atlantic coast (~100m). Sedimentary limestone lowlands with Atlantic maritime climate (mild, wet, year-round grass growth). Primary fat terroir role: midpoint in the CLA butter natural experiment between alpine RSU-36 (~1200m, high CLA) and Nordic lowland RSU-43 (~30m, low CLA). The year-round grass-fed system enabled by the mild maritime climate is the key terroir variable. Dulse seaweed adds one of the highest plant-sourced glutamate values in the dataset. The combination of grass-fed butter fat signal and seaweed umami signal makes this RSU metabolically distinct.
